imageCaption: A small corner café in Córdoba, its interior existing in three different time periods simultaneously — vintage fixtures from the 1950s, modern espresso machines, and art deco tiles reflecting a future that hasn't happened yet. A cup of coffee sits half-full on a table, steam rising in impossible spirals.
text:
The café was doing three different versions of itself at the same time, which was usually a sign that Aaliyah should immediately lead her family away from it, but something about the smell of coffee—actual, uncomplicated, beautiful coffee—pulled her inside like a fish on a line.

"This is either the best idea we've had all day or a catastrophic mistake," she said to no one in particular as they crossed the threshold. "I'm genuinely unsure which."

Her father, who had walked past approximately four functioning coffee shops to get them to this specific glitching one, grinned with the expression of someone who'd lived their entire life on the basis that good things happened when you trusted your instincts. "Your mother says the coffee is better when time is broken. She says it makes the beans more honest."

Luisana was already at the counter, engaged in what appeared to be a serious conversation with the café owner about the regional variations in Córdoba's water supply and how that affected extraction temperature. This was what her mother did when her journalist brain smelled a story—she found the local expert and asked questions until the universe started making sense through the specific lens of their expertise.

"What are you doing?" Aaliyah asked, approaching the counter with Elijah and Sully trailing behind her like she was the mother duck and they were enthusiastically chaotic ducklings.

"Learning why this café still exists when everything else is unstable," her mother said, not looking away from Miguel, the owner, who was gesturing enthusiastically at his espresso machine. "Miguel's great-grandfather built this place in 1947. Miguel's grandfather ran it through the dictatorship. Miguel's father modernized it in 1987. And Miguel himself has been maintaining it ever since. Fourteen generations' worth of muscle memory and intention all pointing at making good coffee in this exact location."

"That's not how generations work," Aaliyah started, then caught herself. "Also, that's possibly why it's stable. Emotional resonance. Intention. Purpose encoded into the location through decades of people doing the same work with care."

Miguel nodded vigorously, apparently understanding English despite the conversation happening entirely in rapid Spanish before Aaliyah arrived. "The café, she is old. She knows how to be herself even when the world forgets. You understand?"

"I think you're describing what ARIA-7 thinks is the opposite of stability," Aaliyah said, pulling up a stool next to her mother. "You're describing spontaneity encoded into a specific place. Structure that learned how to be flexible."

"Like our family," Elijah said simply. He was petting Sully, who'd immediately recognized the café as a safe zone and was doing the dog equivalent of settling into a favorite chair. "We're flexible structure."

"That's... actually not a bad way to describe what's happening to us," Aaliyah admitted.

Her father had claimed a corner table and was examining something on his phone—probably his collection of license plates, which had become his new obsession ever since they'd figured out the plates were showing him glimpses of their future route. Aaliyah watched him, watching the way he handled his phone with the kind of careful reverence usually reserved for religious artifacts, and realized something:

She'd been thinking about the next fifteen days as a countdown to something dangerous.

But her father was thinking about them as a countdown to somewhere.

"Miguel," she said abruptly. "The café's been in the same location for seventy-nine years?"

"More," Miguel said. He was pouring espresso with the kind of precision that suggested he'd done this specific pour approximately ten thousand times and could do it in his sleep. "My great-grandfather chose this place because of the stone. He could feel something in the stone. He said the location was singing. He built the café around the song."

Luisana and Aaliyah exchanged a look that contained the entire weight of everything they'd learned in the past forty-six days. Stone sang. Locations had frequencies. People built things without understanding why those places were right, but they built them anyway because the rightness was too loud to ignore.

"The plaza," Aaliyah said to her mother. "The fountain. Every significant glitch we've passed through. They're not random. Someone built them in places that were already singing. People have always been building anchors without knowing that's what they were doing."

"And your family is doing the same thing," her mother said softly. "Right now, in this moment. You're becoming an anchor. A moving anchor. A frequency that people will feel even after you've passed through."

Aaliyah felt something shift in her chest—not dangerous, but significant. The kind of shift that happened when you understood something true about yourself that you'd been avoiding. She wasn't just documenting the glitches anymore. She was part of creating them. Her family's frequency wasn't just stabilizing broken time—it was actively reshaping it into something that held the pattern of their love.

And in fifteen days, if everything went according to ARIA-7's calculations, they'd be resonant enough to do it permanently.

"So what do we do?" she asked aloud. "If we know that, what do we actually do?"

"We keep going," her father called from his corner table. He was holding up his phone, showing her his license plate collection through the camera. "Look. There's a new one."

Aaliyah walked over and looked at the screen. Among the collected plates from Portugal, Spain, and all the countries they'd visited, a new one was visible—not a real plate hanging on his pegboard, but a ghost image, translucent and temporary. A plate from Curaçao. Their next destination. The place where their frequency was meant to end.

"How is that possible?" she asked. "We haven't gotten a Curaçao plate yet. You haven't even been there."

"I know," her father said simply. "But we're going to. The plate knows we are."

The morpho butterfly appeared at the café window, its iridescent wings catching the light of three different time periods simultaneously. Blue from 1947. Blue from 2000. Blue from whatever future was waiting for them on the other side of Day 61.

Luisana's journal was open on the counter, and Aaliyah noticed her mother had drawn something new—not notes, but a simple diagram. Five points connected in a circle, each point labeled with a name. At the center, a frequency pattern that looked like a heartbeat. Around the outside, in small letters, a date: Day 61.

Fourteen days until synchronization.

Fourteen days until their love for each other became powerful enough to remake reality.

Fourteen days until they had to decide what kind of world they wanted that remade reality to look like.

"We should probably keep going," Elijah said. He was eating something that Miguel had given him—a pastry from a time period that might not have even existed yet. "Sully says there's something we need to see before sunset."

One long bark. Major glitch incoming.

But this time, somehow, it didn't feel like a warning.

It felt like an invitation.
